options.A: Fever in the first 48 hours is most often an inflammatory (cytokine) response, whereas fever ≥72 hours after surgery is more likely infectious
options.B: Fever in the first 48 hours is almost always due to a wound infection
options.C: Fever is never caused by the surgical inflammatory response itself
options.D: Late fever (>72 h) is most often simple atelectasis

exp: Fever in the first 48 hours is most often an inflammatory cytokine response to surgery, whereas fever ≥72 hours later is more likely infectious (wound, urine, pneumonia).
hy: Early fever (<48 h) = inflammatory; late (≥72 h) = infectious.

stem: SIRS is diagnosed by two or more of which set of criteria?

options.A: Temperature >38°C or <36°C, HR >90/min, RR >20/min (or PaCO₂ <32), WBC >12,000 or <4,000 or >10% bands
options.B: Temperature >39°C, HR >110/min, lactate >4 mmol/L, and hypotension
options.C: Two positive blood cultures plus a fever
options.D: A SOFA score increase of ≥2 points

exp: SIRS requires ≥2 of: temperature >38°C or <36°C, HR >90/min, RR >20/min (or PaCO₂ <32), and WBC >12,000 or <4,000 or >10% bands.
hy: SIRS = ≥2 of temp, HR >90, RR >20/PaCO₂ <32, WBC derangement.

stem: How does the mechanism of CRBSI differ by catheter dwell time?

options.A: Short-term (<10 days) CRBSI is usually extraluminal from skin flora at the insertion site, whereas long-term (>10 days) is usually endoluminal
options.B: Short-term CRBSI is endoluminal; long-term is extraluminal
options.C: All CRBSI is hematogenous seeding from a distant source
options.D: Dwell time has no relationship to the route of contamination

exp: Short-term CRBSI (<10 days) is usually extraluminal (skin flora at insertion site); long-term (>10 days) is usually endoluminal (hub/biofilm). Vancomycin is empiric for suspected MRSA.
hy: Short-term CRBSI = extraluminal/skin; long-term = endoluminal.

stem: Which statement about postoperative atelectasis is correct?

options.A: It is the most common postoperative respiratory complication and the most common cause of early postoperative fever
options.B: It is the leading cause of fever beginning on postoperative day 5
options.C: It requires antibiotics in all cases
options.D: It is reversed only by reintubation and positive-pressure ventilation

exp: Atelectasis is the most common postoperative respiratory complication and the most common cause of early postoperative fever; it reverses with mobilization, deep breathing, incentive spirometry, and pain control.
hy: Atelectasis = most common respiratory complication and early-fever cause.

stem: VAP is defined as pneumonia developing after how long of endotracheal intubation, and how do early- vs late-onset organisms differ?

options.A: After 48–72 hours; early-onset (<4 days) is usually antibiotic-sensitive, late-onset (>4 days) is frequently drug-resistant
options.B: After 12 hours; early-onset organisms are drug-resistant
options.C: After 7 days; there is no difference between early and late organisms
options.D: After 24 hours; late-onset pneumonia is always due to S. aureus

exp: VAP is pneumonia developing 48–72 hours after intubation; early-onset (<4 days) is usually antibiotic-sensitive, late-onset (>4 days) frequently drug-resistant.
hy: VAP = >48–72 h post-intubation; early sensitive, late resistant.

stem: Aspiration pneumonitis (Mendelson syndrome) becomes more severe above which thresholds of aspirate acidity and volume?

options.A: pH below 2.5 and volume above 20 mL
options.B: pH below 5.0 and volume above 50 mL
options.C: pH above 4.0 and volume above 5 mL
options.D: pH below 1.0 and volume above 100 mL

exp: Mendelson syndrome worsens with more acidic (critical pH <2.5) and more voluminous (>20 mL) aspirate; particulate matter injures independent of acidity.
hy: Aspiration severity rises with pH <2.5 and volume >20 mL.
src: Sabiston Ch 12, "Aspiration Pneumonitis and Aspiration Pneumonia"

stem: Why is the distinction between aspiration pneumonitis and aspiration pneumonia clinically important?

options.A: Pneumonitis (sterile acid injury) is managed supportively, whereas aspiration pneumonia requires antibiotics
options.B: Pneumonitis always requires antibiotics, whereas pneumonia is managed supportively
options.C: Both are treated identically, so the distinction is academic
options.D: Pneumonitis is infectious and pneumonia is sterile

exp: Aspiration pneumonitis is a sterile acid injury managed supportively, whereas aspiration pneumonia is infectious and requires antibiotics — so the distinction changes management.
hy: Pneumonitis = supportive; pneumonia = antibiotics.
src: Sabiston Ch 12, "Aspiration Pneumonitis and Aspiration Pneumonia"

stem: Which classification of insult correctly matches ARDS etiology?

options.A: Direct lung injury — pneumonia, aspiration, chest trauma; indirect — severe sepsis, pancreatitis, ischemia/reperfusion
options.B: Direct lung injury — pancreatitis and sepsis; indirect — pneumonia and aspiration
options.C: ARDS resolves faster than cardiogenic pulmonary edema
options.D: ARDS is characterized by decreased capillary permeability

exp: ARDS results from direct lung injury (pneumonia, aspiration, chest trauma) or indirect injury (sepsis, pancreatitis, shock, ischemia/reperfusion); it shows increased capillary permeability and resolves slowly.
hy: ARDS: direct (pneumonia/aspiration) vs indirect (sepsis/pancreatitis).

stem: According to the POISE trial, what proportion of patients with perioperative myocardial infarction do not experience ischemic symptoms?

options.A: About 10%
options.B: About 35%
options.D: About 90%

exp: Per POISE, about 65% of perioperative myocardial infarctions are clinically silent, which is why symptom-based diagnosis underdetects PMI; troponin elevation independently predicts mortality.
hy: ~65% of perioperative MIs are clinically silent (POISE).
src: Sabiston Ch 12, "Perioperative Myocardial Ischemia and Infarction"

stem: What is the 2014 ACC/AHA position on routinely screening surgical patients with troponin levels?

options.A: Routine screening is not recommended; troponin should be checked only in patients with signs/symptoms suggestive of myocardial infarction
options.B: Every surgical patient should have troponin checked before and 72 h after surgery
options.C: Troponin should replace the ECG for perioperative cardiac assessment
options.D: Troponin is unaffected by renal failure, heart failure, or atrial fibrillation, making it an ideal universal screen

exp: The 2014 ACC/AHA guidance recommends against routine troponin screening — check only if signs/symptoms suggest MI — because troponin rises nonspecifically in renal failure, heart failure, PE, and atrial fibrillation.
hy: No routine troponin screening — only if symptomatic.
src: Sabiston Ch 12, "Perioperative Myocardial Ischemia and Infarction"

stem: How many RCRI risk factors place a patient at increased risk for major perioperative cardiac complications?

options.A: One or more
options.B: Two or more
options.D: All six

exp: Two or more RCRI risk factors indicate increased risk for major perioperative cardiac complications. The six are high-risk surgery, ischemic heart disease, CHF, cerebrovascular disease, insulin-treated diabetes, and creatinine >2 mg/dL.
hy: RCRI ≥2 factors = elevated cardiac risk.

stem: How is postoperative hypertension defined, and what is the recommended initial blood-pressure target when treating it?

options.A: BP >190/100 mm Hg on two consecutive readings; lower BP by about 25% then reassess
options.B: BP >140/90 mm Hg once; normalize BP completely and immediately
options.C: BP >160/95 mm Hg once; lower BP by 50% within 10 minutes
options.D: BP >200/120 mm Hg sustained for 1 hour; no treatment is needed

exp: Postoperative hypertension is BP >190/100 mm Hg on two consecutive readings; treat with rapid-onset short-acting agents aiming to lower BP by ~25% then reassess.
hy: Postop HTN = >190/100 ×2; lower BP ~25% then reassess.

stem: Which statement about postoperative atrial fibrillation is correct?

options.A: It commonly develops within 3 days of surgery; rate control is first-line in stable patients, and the CHA₂DS₂-VASc score (anticoagulate if ≥2) guides thromboembolic prophylaxis
options.B: Immediate electrical cardioversion is first-line in all hemodynamically stable patients
options.C: Anticoagulation is mandatory for every episode regardless of duration or risk score
options.D: Beta blockers are contraindicated for rate control after surgery

exp: Postoperative AF/flutter usually appears within 3 days; rate control is first-line in stable patients, and the CHA₂DS₂-VASc score (anticoagulate if ≥2) guides prophylaxis; cardioversion is reserved for instability.
hy: Postop AF within 3 d; rate control first; anticoagulate if CHA₂DS₂-VASc ≥2.

stem: On the day of surgery, which medication-management statement is correct?

options.A: ACE inhibitors/ARBs are often held the day before/of surgery to prevent intraoperative hypotension, while chronic beta blockers are continued
options.B: Beta blockers should be stopped the morning of surgery to avoid bradycardia
options.C: ACE inhibitors should be newly started the morning of surgery in all patients
options.D: All antihypertensives should be withheld for 48 hours before surgery

exp: ACE inhibitors/ARBs are often held the day before/of surgery to prevent intraoperative hypotension, while chronic beta blockers are continued to avoid a catecholamine surge.
hy: Hold ACE/ARB day of surgery; continue chronic beta blockers.

stem: Which statement about postoperative heart failure is supported by the chapter?

options.A: Perioperative heart failure is associated with a ~63% increased mortality; diastolic (preserved-EF) failure carries a lower mortality than systolic (reduced-EF, especially EF <30%)
options.B: Diastolic heart failure carries a higher mortality than systolic heart failure
options.C: BNP has no role in perioperative risk assessment
options.D: Heart failure is not included in any cardiac risk-stratification system

exp: Perioperative heart failure carries ~63% increased mortality; diastolic (preserved-EF) failure has lower mortality than systolic (reduced-EF, especially EF <30%).
hy: Perioperative HF ~63% higher mortality; systolic worse than diastolic.

stem: In the cited meta-analysis of vascular surgery patients, a preoperative BNP cut point of 30 pg/mL had which test characteristics for predicting 30-day major adverse cardiac events?

options.A: ~95% sensitivity and ~44% specificity
options.B: ~44% sensitivity and ~95% specificity
options.D: ~50% sensitivity and ~50% specificity

exp: A preoperative BNP cut point of 30 pg/mL gave ~95% sensitivity and ~44% specificity for 30-day MACE in vascular surgery patients — a sensitive but nonspecific screen.
hy: BNP 30 pg/mL ≈ 95% sensitive, 44% specific.

stem: By the 2012 KDIGO criteria, which finding meets the definition of acute kidney injury?

options.A: A rise in serum creatinine of ≥0.3 mg/dL within 48 hours
options.B: A rise in serum creatinine of ≥0.3 mg/dL within 30 days
options.D: Any single creatinine value above 1.5 mg/dL

exp: KDIGO defines AKI as a creatinine rise ≥0.3 mg/dL within 48 hours, OR ≥1.5× baseline within 7 days, OR urine output <0.5 mL/kg/h for 6 hours.
hy: KDIGO AKI: ΔCr ≥0.3 in 48 h, or ≥1.5× in 7 d, or UOP <0.5 ×6 h.

stem: Which KDIGO stage-3 AKI criterion is correct?

options.A: Serum creatinine ≥3 times baseline (or ≥4.0 mg/dL), or urine output <0.3 mL/kg/h for ≥24 h, or anuria for ≥12 h
options.B: Serum creatinine 1.5–1.9 times baseline
options.D: Serum creatinine 2.0–2.9 times baseline

exp: KDIGO stage 3 = creatinine ≥3× baseline (or ≥4.0 mg/dL), or urine output <0.3 mL/kg/h for ≥24 h, or anuria ≥12 h (or RRT). Stage 1 is 1.5–1.9× / ≥0.3 mg/dL; stage 2 is 2.0–2.9×.
hy: KDIGO stage 3: ≥3× baseline / Cr ≥4.0 / UOP <0.3 ×24 h.

stem: Which laboratory pattern indicates prerenal (rather than intrinsic renal) azotemia?

options.A: Urine osmolality >500 mOsm/L, urinary sodium <20, fractional excretion of sodium <1%, BUN:creatinine >20
options.B: Urine osmolality equal to plasma, urinary sodium >50, FeNa >3%
options.D: Urinary sodium >50 mOsm/L with isosthenuric urine

exp: Prerenal azotemia: concentrated urine (osmolality >500), avid sodium reabsorption (urinary Na <20, FeNa <1%), and high BUN:creatinine (>20). Intrinsic renal failure shows the opposite.
hy: Prerenal: U-osm >500, U-Na <20, FeNa <1%, BUN:Cr >20.

stem: Which factor is listed as a risk factor for developing postoperative acute kidney injury?

options.A: Preexisting chronic kidney disease and intraoperative hypotension
options.B: Young age and low ASA class
options.D: Laparoscopic approach

exp: AKI risk factors include preexisting CKD and intraoperative hypotension (plus age, obesity, diabetes, hypertension, anemia, transfusion, high ASA, heart failure, nephrotoxins, cardiac/emergency surgery).
hy: AKI risks: CKD, hypotension, contrast/nephrotoxins, cardiac/emergency surgery.

stem: Which statement about renal (intrinsic) causes of perioperative AKI is correct?

options.A: NSAIDs, aminoglycosides, amphotericin B, and contrast media are directly nephrotoxic, and rhabdomyolysis injures tubules via myoglobin
options.B: Contrast media protect the renal medulla from ischemia
options.D: Rhabdomyolysis causes a purely postrenal (obstructive) injury

exp: Intrinsic causes include nephrotoxins (NSAIDs, aminoglycosides, amphotericin B, contrast) and rhabdomyolysis, in which myoglobin injures tubules. Contrast and aminoglycosides are toxic, not protective.
hy: Renal AKI: NSAIDs/aminoglycosides/ampho B/contrast + myoglobin.

stem: When postoperative urinary retention is suspected and bladder ultrasound is performed, what bladder volume generally warrants intervention?
exp: A bladder volume of ≥500 mL on ultrasound warrants intervention (catheterization; suprapubic if urethral stricture/trauma).
hy: Bladder ≥500 mL → catheterize.

stem: Which feature distinguishes primary adrenal insufficiency (Addison disease) from central (secondary) adrenal insufficiency?

options.A: Primary AI causes hyperpigmentation, hyponatremia, and hyperkalemia (mineralocorticoid lost); central AI spares mineralocorticoids and lacks hyperpigmentation
options.B: Central AI causes hyperpigmentation; primary AI does not
options.C: Primary AI spares aldosterone, so potassium is normal
options.D: Central AI characteristically causes hyperkalemia

exp: Primary AI (Addison) loses aldosterone (hyponatremia, hyperkalemia, hypovolemia) and high ACTH causes hyperpigmentation; central AI spares mineralocorticoids and has no hyperpigmentation.
hy: Primary AI: hyperpigmentation + hyperkalemia; central spares both.

stem: Which patient is at low risk for HPA-axis suppression and does not require perioperative stress-dose steroids?

options.A: A patient on prednisolone 5–10 mg/day for less than 3 weeks
options.B: A patient on >20 mg/day prednisone for more than 3 weeks
options.C: A patient with a cushingoid appearance
options.D: A patient with a morning cortisol <5 mcg/dL

exp: Low-dose steroids (~5–10 mg/day prednisolone) for <3 weeks pose low HPA-suppression risk and need no stress dosing or testing. High dose >3 weeks, cushingoid appearance, or AM cortisol <5 indicate suppression.
hy: Low-dose steroids <3 weeks → no stress dose.

stem: What is the initial pharmacologic management of suspected perioperative adrenal crisis?

options.A: IV hydrocortisone 100 mg bolus with 1 L normal saline, plus correction of electrolytes and glucose
options.B: Oral prednisone 5 mg and observation
options.C: Fluid restriction and a vasopressin infusion
options.D: Withholding steroids until a stimulation test confirms the diagnosis

exp: Adrenal crisis is treated with IV hydrocortisone 100 mg bolus plus 1 L normal saline and correction of electrolytes/glucose/pH; do not delay treatment for stimulation testing.
hy: Adrenal crisis: IV hydrocortisone 100 mg + saline now.

stem: Using the Burch-Wartofsky scoring system, which score is "highly suggestive" of thyroid storm, and what is the untreated mortality?

options.A: A score ≥45 is highly suggestive; untreated mortality is ~20%–50%
options.B: A score ≥25 is highly suggestive; mortality is <5%
options.D: A score of 10 confirms thyroid storm with ~90% mortality

exp: On Burch-Wartofsky, <25 is unlikely, 25–44 impending, and ≥45 highly suggestive of thyroid storm; untreated mortality is ~20%–50%.
hy: Burch-Wartofsky ≥45 highly suggestive; storm mortality ~20–50%.

stem: Which statement about thyroid-storm pharmacotherapy is correct?

options.A: Thionamides (propylthiouracil/methimazole) block synthesis; potassium iodide blocks hormone release; steroids reduce T4→T3 conversion; acetaminophen (not aspirin) is used for fever
options.B: Aspirin is the preferred antipyretic because it lowers free thyroid hormone
options.C: Potassium iodide should be given before thionamides to block synthesis
options.D: Beta blockers are contraindicated in thyroid storm

exp: Storm therapy: thionamides (PTU/methimazole) block synthesis, potassium iodide blocks release (given after thionamide), steroids reduce T4→T3 conversion, beta blockers control rate, and acetaminophen — not aspirin — is used for fever.
hy: Storm: thionamide then iodide; steroids; acetaminophen not aspirin.

stem: Which statement about myxedema coma is correct?

options.A: It carries a mortality of ~40%–50% and is treated with IV thyroid hormone (T4 ± T3) plus IV hydrocortisone and supportive care
options.B: It is treated with oral levothyroxine and discharge home
options.C: Steroids are contraindicated because cortisol levels are elevated
options.D: It presents with hyperthermia and tachycardia

exp: Myxedema coma (mortality ~40%–50%) is treated with IV thyroid hormone (T4 ± T3), IV hydrocortisone (concurrent adrenal insufficiency), ventilation, rewarming, and correction of hyponatremia.
hy: Myxedema coma: IV T4 ± T3 + IV hydrocortisone + support.

stem: In SIADH, at approximately what serum sodium do significant neurologic symptoms appear, and what is the maximum safe correction rate?

options.A: Significant symptoms <125 mEq/L; correct no faster than ~8 mEq/L per 24 h to avoid osmotic demyelination
options.B: Significant symptoms <140 mEq/L; correct as fast as possible
options.C: Significant symptoms <115 mEq/L; correct at 5 mEq/L per hour
options.D: Symptoms appear only below 100 mEq/L; correction rate is irrelevant

exp: In SIADH, significant neurologic symptoms appear below ~125 mEq/L, and correction must not exceed ~8 mEq/L per 24 h to avoid osmotic demyelination.
hy: SIADH: symptoms <125 mEq/L; correct ≤8 mEq/L/24 h.

stem: Which is the most common tumor cause of SIADH listed in the chapter?

options.A: Small cell lung cancer
options.B: Pancreatic adenocarcinoma
options.D: Colorectal cancer

exp: Small cell lung cancer is the most common tumor cause of SIADH.
hy: Small cell lung cancer = most common tumor cause of SIADH.

stem: After abdominal surgery, in what order does motility typically return?

options.A: Small bowel within hours, stomach in 24–48 hours, colon in 3–5 days
options.B: Colon first (hours), then stomach, then small bowel (days)
options.D: Stomach within hours, small bowel in 3–5 days, colon last at 10 days

exp: Motility returns small bowel within hours, stomach 24–48 hours, colon 3–5 days; the colon is rate-limiting for ileus resolution.
hy: Motility: small bowel (hours) → stomach (24–48 h) → colon (3–5 d).
src: Sabiston Ch 12, "Ileus and Early Postoperative Bowel Obstruction"

stem: Early postoperative mechanical bowel obstruction is defined as occurring within what interval, and what is its most common cause?

options.A: Within 6 weeks of surgery; adhesions (>90%)
options.B: Within 1 week of surgery; internal hernia
options.C: Within 6 months of surgery; anastomotic stricture
options.D: Within 24 hours of surgery; intussusception

exp: Early postoperative mechanical obstruction occurs within 6 weeks, and adhesions cause >90% of cases.
hy: Early postop obstruction = <6 weeks; >90% adhesions.
src: Sabiston Ch 12, "Ileus and Early Postoperative Bowel Obstruction"

stem: Which presentation is most consistent with a high (proximal) small-bowel obstruction relative to a distal one?

options.A: Early, prominent bilious vomiting with less distention
options.B: Late feculent vomiting with marked distention
options.C: Painless distention with continued passage of flatus
options.D: Episodes spaced minutes apart with massive distention

exp: A high (proximal) small-bowel obstruction presents early with bilious vomiting and little distention; a distal obstruction causes late feculent vomiting and marked distention.
hy: Proximal SBO: early bilious vomiting, little distention.
src: Sabiston Ch 12, "Ileus and Early Postoperative Bowel Obstruction"

stem: Abdominal compartment syndrome is defined as a sustained intraabdominal pressure at or above which value, with new organ dysfunction that improves after decompression?

options.A: ≥12 mm Hg
options.B: ≥5 mm Hg
options.D: ≥50 mm Hg

exp: Abdominal compartment syndrome is a sustained IAP ≥12 mm Hg with new organ dysfunction reversed by decompression.
hy: ACS = sustained IAP ≥12 mm Hg + new organ dysfunction.

stem: Which statement about abdominal compartment syndrome is correct?

options.A: Intraabdominal pressure is most commonly estimated via bladder (transurethral) pressure, and definitive treatment of established ACS is decompressive laparotomy
options.B: ACS is diagnosed only by direct intraperitoneal pressure transducers
options.C: Diuresis is the definitive treatment for established ACS
options.D: Sudden decompression has no hemodynamic consequences

exp: IAP is most commonly estimated via bladder (transurethral) pressure, and definitive treatment of established ACS is decompressive laparotomy; sudden decompression can cause reperfusion acidosis/hypotension.
hy: Measure IAP via bladder pressure; treat with decompressive laparotomy.

stem: Upper GI bleeding is anatomically defined as hemorrhage proximal to which landmark, and what is its most common cause?

options.A: The ligament of Treitz; peptic ulcer disease
options.B: The ileocecal valve; diverticulosis
options.D: The hepatic flexure; Mallory-Weiss tear

exp: Upper GI bleeding is proximal to the ligament of Treitz; peptic ulcer disease is the most common cause.
hy: UGIB = proximal to ligament of Treitz; PUD most common.

stem: Which eponymous stress ulcer is correctly matched to its setting?

options.A: Curling ulcer — burns; Cushing ulcer — raised intracranial pressure
options.B: Curling ulcer — head injury; Cushing ulcer — burns
options.C: Cushing ulcer — sepsis; Curling ulcer — NSAID use
options.D: Both occur only in ambulatory outpatients

exp: Curling ulcer = burns; Cushing ulcer = raised intracranial pressure (head injury/neurosurgery).
hy: Curling = burns; Cushing = raised ICP.

stem: Which patients are at highest risk for stress-related mucosal bleeding, and what is the intragastric pH goal?

options.A: Mechanically ventilated and coagulopathic patients; keep intraluminal pH above 3.5
options.B: Ambulatory patients on a normal diet; keep pH below 2
options.C: Only patients with known peptic ulcer disease; pH goal is irrelevant
options.D: Patients with low intracranial pressure; keep pH below 1

exp: Mechanically ventilated and coagulopathic patients are at highest risk for stress-related bleeding and warrant prophylaxis; keep intragastric pH above 3.5.
hy: Ventilation + coagulopathy = highest stress-ulcer risk; pH >3.5.

stem: Which stomal complication is correctly classified as early (vs late)?

options.A: Ischemic necrosis and retraction are early; parastomal hernia and stenosis are late
options.B: Parastomal hernia and stenosis are early; ischemia is late
options.C: Prolapse is an early complication and ischemia is late
options.D: All stomal complications are classified as late

exp: Early stomal complications include ischemic necrosis, retraction, and detachment; late complications include parastomal hernia, stenosis, prolapse, and fistula.
hy: Early stoma: ischemia/retraction; late: hernia/stenosis/prolapse.

stem: A congested stoma is dark purple on postoperative day 0. How is viability best assessed at the bedside, and which finding indicates a viable stoma?

options.A: Transillumination — a viable stoma transilluminates bright red even with venous congestion; failure to transilluminate indicates ischemia
options.B: A black stoma always indicates necrosis requiring immediate laparotomy regardless of depth
options.C: A dark stoma that bleeds on pinprick must be revised emergently
options.D: Doppler of the abdominal aorta confirms stomal viability

exp: A viable stoma transilluminates bright red even with venous congestion; failure to transilluminate indicates ischemia, and the key issue is whether ischemia extends below the fascia.
hy: Stoma transilluminates bright red = viable.

stem: Which set of antibiotics is most classically associated with precipitating Clostridioides difficile infection?

options.A: Clindamycin, fluoroquinolones, and broad-spectrum penicillins/cephalosporins
options.B: Topical mupirocin and bacitracin
options.D: Nystatin and oral vancomycin exclusively

exp: Clindamycin, fluoroquinolones, and broad-spectrum penicillins/cephalosporins are the classic precipitants of C. difficile infection by disrupting gut flora (nearly all antibiotics can).
hy: Clindamycin, fluoroquinolones, broad-spectrum β-lactams precipitate C. diff.

stem: Which laboratory pattern indicates severe (rather than non-severe) C. difficile infection?

options.A: Leukocytosis >15,000/µL and serum creatinine >1.5 mg/dL
options.B: Leukocytosis ≤15,000/µL and creatinine <1.5 mg/dL
options.C: A normal white count with hypoalbuminemia alone
options.D: Eosinophilia with a normal creatinine

exp: Severe C. difficile infection = leukocytosis >15,000/µL and serum creatinine >1.5 mg/dL; hypotension, ileus, or toxic megacolon define fulminant disease.
hy: Severe C. diff: WBC >15,000 and Cr >1.5.

stem: For fulminant or refractory C. difficile colitis requiring an operation, which procedures are described?

options.A: Total abdominal colectomy with end ileostomy, or a colon-sparing diverting loop ileostomy with antegrade vancomycin lavage
options.B: Segmental sigmoid resection with primary anastomosis
options.D: Subtotal gastrectomy

exp: Operative options for fulminant/refractory CDI are total abdominal colectomy with end ileostomy, or a colon-sparing diverting loop ileostomy with antegrade vancomycin lavage.
hy: Surgery for fulminant C. diff: total colectomy + ileostomy, or loop ileostomy + vanco lavage.

stem: Regarding anastomotic location and leak risk, which statement is correct?

options.A: Small-bowel (enteroenteric/ileocolic) anastomoses are low-risk, whereas esophageal, pancreaticoenteric, and low colorectal anastomoses are high-risk; a colorectal anastomosis below the peritoneal reflection leaks in ~8%–20%
options.B: Ileocolic anastomoses have the highest leak rate of any GI anastomosis
options.C: Esophageal anastomoses are the lowest-risk anastomoses
options.D: Anastomotic location has no bearing on leak rate

exp: Small-bowel/ileocolic anastomoses are low-risk; esophageal, pancreaticoenteric, and colorectal are high-risk; a colorectal anastomosis below the peritoneal reflection leaks in ~8%–20%.
hy: High-risk: esophageal/pancreatic/low colorectal (8–20% below peritoneal reflection).

stem: Below what arterial oxygen tension does the chapter say mature collagen formation (and thus anastomotic healing) becomes impaired?

options.A: Below 35 mm Hg (with angiogenesis/epithelialization further impaired below 10 mm Hg)
options.B: Below 80 mm Hg
options.D: Below 100 mm Hg

exp: Mature collagen formation fails below 35 mm Hg arterial O₂ tension; angiogenesis/epithelialization fail below 10 mm Hg — hence maintain euvolemia/normoxia.
hy: Collagen maturation fails <35 mm Hg; angiogenesis <10 mm Hg.

stem: Which statement about the timing of anastomotic leak presentation is correct?

options.A: Gastrojejunal leaks after gastric bypass tend to present early (<24 h), while small-bowel and colon leaks typically present 5–7 days postoperatively
options.B: All anastomotic leaks present on postoperative day 1
options.C: Colon leaks always present before gastrojejunal leaks
options.D: Leaks never present after postoperative day 3

exp: Gastrojejunal leaks present early (<24 h); small-bowel and colon leaks typically present 5–7 days postoperatively.
hy: Gastrojejunal leaks <24 h; small-bowel/colon 5–7 days.

stem: Enterocutaneous fistulas are classified by 24-hour output. Which set of cut points is correct?

options.A: Low <200 mL/day, moderate 200–500 mL/day, high >500 mL/day
options.B: Low <50 mL/day, moderate 50–100 mL/day, high >100 mL/day
options.D: Output does not affect management

exp: Enterocutaneous fistula output: low <200 mL/day, moderate 200–500, high >500; output is the key determinant of physiologic impact and management.
hy: ECF output: low <200, moderate 200–500, high >500 mL/day.

stem: Which set of features favors spontaneous closure of an enterocutaneous fistula?

options.A: A long tract (>2 cm), low output, no distal obstruction, no adjacent abscess, and absence of intrinsic bowel disease
options.B: A short tract (<2 cm) with an epithelialized tract
options.C: High output with an associated large abscess
options.D: Origin from the lateral duodenum with distal obstruction

exp: Favorable features for spontaneous ECF closure: long tract (>2 cm), low output, no distal obstruction, no adjacent abscess, small enteral defect, and no intrinsic bowel disease. Most close within ~4 weeks.
hy: Favorable ECF: long tract, low output, no obstruction/abscess.

stem: The ISGPF definition of a clinically relevant postoperative pancreatic fistula requires drain fluid amylase above what level, and which gland features predict high risk?

options.A: Amylase >3× the upper limit of normal; a soft gland and a small duct (≤3 mm)
options.B: Amylase >1.5× normal; a hard gland and a dilated duct
options.D: Any measurable drain output regardless of amylase, only with a firm gland

exp: A clinically relevant POPF is drain amylase >3× the upper limit of normal; a soft gland and small duct (≤3 mm) are high-risk features.
hy: POPF: drain amylase >3× ULN; soft gland + small duct = high risk.

stem: Which statement about bile duct injury during cholecystectomy is correct?

options.A: The common bile duct is most often injured when mistaken for the cystic duct; the "critical view of safety" and selective cholangiography reduce risk; major BDI occurs in ~3/1000 cases
options.B: Major bile duct injury occurs in roughly 1 in 10 cholecystectomies
options.C: The infundibular technique is the most reliable way to avoid injury
options.D: Open cholecystectomy injuries are typically more proximal and complex than laparoscopic ones

exp: The CBD is most often injured when mistaken for the cystic duct; the critical view of safety (with selective cholangiography) reduces risk; major BDI occurs in ~3/1000 cholecystectomies, and laparoscopic injuries tend to be more proximal/complex than open.
hy: CBD mistaken for cystic duct; critical view of safety; major BDI ~3/1000.

stem: Which statement about vasculobiliary injury is correct?

options.A: The right hepatic artery is involved in ~90% of cases; extreme injuries involving the portal vein/proper hepatic artery can cause hepatic infarction with ~50% mortality
options.B: The left hepatic artery is involved in ~90% of cases
options.C: Vasculobiliary injury carries no higher mortality than isolated bile duct injury
options.D: Nonenhancement of the right lobe on CT excludes hepatic artery injury

exp: The right hepatic artery is involved in ~90% of vasculobiliary injuries; extreme injuries to the portal vein/proper hepatic artery can cause hepatic infarction with ~50% mortality.
hy: Vasculobiliary injury: right hepatic artery ~90%; extreme ~50% mortality.

stem: Which statement about postoperative delirium is correct?

options.A: Incidence is ~15%–53% in elderly surgical patients; the hypoactive subtype is the most common and the most often under-diagnosed
options.B: The hyperactive subtype is the most common and easiest to miss
options.C: Delirium is, by definition, an irreversible cognitive decline
options.D: It characteristically presents 3 weeks after discharge

exp: Postoperative delirium occurs in ~15%–53% of elderly surgical patients; the hypoactive subtype is the most common and most often missed; delirium is typically reversible/fluctuating.
hy: POD 15–53%; hypoactive type most common and most under-diagnosed.

stem: Which medication strategy is recommended in managing postoperative delirium?

options.A: Avoid benzodiazepines and anticholinergics; reserve low-dose antipsychotics (e.g., haloperidol) for hyperactive, dangerous agitation, using benzodiazepines only for alcohol/benzodiazepine withdrawal
options.B: Routine scheduled benzodiazepines for all delirious patients
options.C: First-line physical restraints and deep sedation
options.D: Anticholinergic agents to "dry up" secretions and calm the patient

exp: Avoid benzodiazepines and anticholinergics; use nonpharmacologic measures first, with low-dose antipsychotics reserved for hyperactive dangerous agitation and benzodiazepines only for alcohol/benzodiazepine withdrawal.
hy: Avoid benzos/anticholinergics; antipsychotics only for dangerous hyperactivity.

stem: Which statement about perioperative seizures is correct?

options.A: IV benzodiazepines (lorazepam/diazepam) are first-line to abort a seizure, and prophylactic antiepileptics in patients with no seizure history are not beneficial
options.B: Prophylactic phenytoin should be started in all surgical patients
options.C: Phenobarbital is the first-line agent to abort an acute seizure
options.D: Antiepileptic drugs should be stopped abruptly before surgery in known epileptics

exp: IV benzodiazepines (lorazepam/diazepam) are first-line to abort a seizure; prophylactic antiepileptics in patients with no seizure history are not beneficial.
hy: Benzodiazepines abort seizures; no prophylactic AED if no history.

stem: Which statement about perioperative stroke is correct?

options.A: It is defined within 30 days of surgery; ischemic strokes predominate (hemorrhagic ~1%–4%); elective surgery should be delayed ~6–12 months after a recent stroke; thrombolysis is a relative contraindication after major surgery
options.B: Hemorrhagic strokes account for the majority of perioperative strokes
options.C: tPA is the routine first-line therapy immediately after major surgery
options.D: Elective surgery may proceed the week after an acute stroke without added risk

exp: Perioperative stroke is defined within 30 days; ischemic predominate (hemorrhagic ~1%–4%); delay elective surgery ~6–12 months after a recent stroke; thrombolysis is a relative contraindication after major surgery.
hy: Stroke ≤30 d; delay elective 6–12 mo; tPA relatively contraindicated.

stem: Which statement about postoperative epistaxis is correct?

options.A: Anterior epistaxis (often from the readily accessible anterior septum) is usually controlled by pinching the cartilaginous septum; posterior epistaxis (sphenopalatine vessels, more common in elderly) requires posterior packing or embolization
options.B: Posterior epistaxis is controlled by simple nasal pinching
options.C: Anterior epistaxis arises from the sphenopalatine artery
options.D: Posterior epistaxis is more common in young children than the elderly

exp: Anterior epistaxis (anterior septum/Kiesselbach) is controlled by pinching the cartilaginous septum; posterior epistaxis (sphenopalatine vessels, elderly) needs posterior packing or embolization.
hy: Anterior = pinch; posterior (sphenopalatine, elderly) = packing/embolization.

stem: Which factor most strongly predisposes a critically ill patient to nosocomial sinusitis, and how is it diagnosed?

options.A: Nasal instrumentation (nasotracheal or nasogastric tubes) obstructing the ostiomeatal complex; CT showing opacification/air-fluid levels/≥4 mm mucosal thickening
options.B: Oral (orotracheal) intubation; diagnosis by chest radiograph
options.C: Early mobilization; diagnosis by blood culture alone
options.D: Low Glasgow coma scores are protective against sinusitis

exp: Nasal instrumentation (nasotracheal/nasogastric tubes) obstructs the ostiomeatal complex and predisposes to nosocomial sinusitis; CT shows opacification, air-fluid levels, or ≥4 mm mucosal thickening.
hy: Nosocomial sinusitis: nasal tubes; CT shows opacification/≥4 mm thickening.

stem: Acute suppurative parotitis in a debilitated postoperative patient is which type of infection, and what is its feared extension?

options.A: Most often Staphylococcus aureus in a dehydrated patient with poor oral hygiene; it can extend to a deep neck space infection (mortality ~20%–40%)
options.B: Most often a viral infection requiring no antibiotics
options.C: Caused by gram-negative anaerobes exclusively, never extending beyond the gland
options.D: A benign, self-limited condition that never requires drainage

exp: Acute suppurative parotitis is usually S. aureus in a dehydrated, debilitated patient with poor oral hygiene; it can extend into a deep neck space infection with ~20%–40% mortality.
hy: Parotitis = S. aureus; can extend to lethal deep neck space infection.

stem: The Fried frailty phenotype defines frailty as the presence of how many of its five features?

options.A: Three or more (one or two = "prefrail")
options.B: One or more
options.D: Two or more

exp: The Fried frailty phenotype = ≥3 of five features (slowness, weakness, exhaustion, weight loss, low activity); one or two = prefrail.
hy: Fried frailty = ≥3 of 5 (prefrail = 1–2).
src: Sabiston Ch 12, "The Geriatric Patient and Frailty"

stem: For a patient on dabigatran, how does the recommended preoperative discontinuation interval depend on renal function?

options.A: It lengthens as renal function falls — about 1–2 days if CrCl ≥50 mL/min, but 3–5 days if CrCl <50 mL/min
options.B: It is a fixed 24 hours regardless of renal function
options.C: It shortens as renal function falls
options.D: Dabigatran never needs to be stopped before surgery

exp: For dabigatran (renally cleared direct thrombin inhibitor), the preoperative hold lengthens with worse renal function: 1–2 days if CrCl ≥50 mL/min, 3–5 days if CrCl <50.
hy: Dabigatran hold: 1–2 d if CrCl ≥50, 3–5 d if <50.

stem: Which set of items are components of the HAS-BLED bleeding-risk score?

options.A: Hypertension (SBP >160), abnormal renal/liver function, prior stroke, bleeding history, labile INR, elderly (>65), and drugs/alcohol
options.B: Age, sex, ASA class, BMI, and smoking status
options.C: Ejection fraction, troponin, BNP, and creatinine clearance only
options.D: Diabetes, vascular disease, and congestive heart failure only

exp: HAS-BLED components: Hypertension (SBP >160), abnormal renal/liver function, stroke history, bleeding history, labile INRs, elderly (>65), and drugs/alcohol.
hy: HAS-BLED = HTN, renal/liver, stroke, bleeding, labile INR, elderly, drugs/alcohol.

stem: A bowel operation with uncontrolled gross spillage from the GI tract and apparent inflammation, but no pre-existing pus, is which wound class with what expected infection rate?

options.A: Contaminated; ~20%–25%
options.B: Clean-contaminated; ~5%–8%
options.D: Clean; ~1%–3%

exp: Gross uncontrolled spillage with inflammation but no pre-existing pus is a contaminated (Class III) wound, ~20%–25% infection. Clean-contaminated is controlled entry without spillage (5–8%); dirty requires established infection/perforation (30–40%).
hy: Gross spillage + inflammation, no prior pus = contaminated, ~20–25%.

stem: Which pathogen profile is expected after a head-and-neck procedure that incises through the oropharyngeal mucosa?

options.A: S. aureus, streptococci, and oropharyngeal anaerobes (e.g., peptostreptococci)
options.B: Gram-negative bacilli and Candida exclusively
options.D: Pseudomonas aeruginosa as the sole organism

exp: Head-and-neck surgery breaching oropharyngeal mucosa is expected to involve S. aureus, streptococci, and oropharyngeal anaerobes (peptostreptococci).
hy: Oropharyngeal-mucosa surgery: S. aureus + streptococci + oral anaerobes.

stem: Which is correctly categorized as a noninfectious cause of postoperative fever?

options.A: Atelectasis, drug reaction, pancreatitis, and pulmonary embolus
options.B: Pseudomembranous colitis
options.D: Catheter device–related infection

exp: Noninfectious fever causes include atelectasis, drug reaction, pancreatitis, pulmonary embolus, adrenal insufficiency, allergic and transfusion reactions. Pseudomembranous colitis, empyema, and device infection are infectious.
hy: Noninfectious fever: atelectasis, drug reaction, pancreatitis, PE, transfusion reaction.

stem: In the cardiac risk-stratification table, which procedure is classified as high cardiac risk (often >5%)?

options.A: Aortic and other major vascular surgery
options.B: Carotid endarterectomy
options.D: Cataract surgery

exp: High cardiac risk (often >5%): aortic/major vascular surgery, peripheral vascular surgery, prolonged operations with large fluid shifts, and emergency major operations in the elderly. CEA is intermediate; breast/cataract are low.
hy: High cardiac risk: aortic/major vascular and emergency major surgery.

stem: Which procedure is classified as intermediate (generally <5%) cardiac risk?

options.A: Carotid endarterectomy / intraperitoneal or intrathoracic surgery
options.B: Endoscopic procedures
options.C: Emergency major operations in the elderly
options.D: Superficial procedures

exp: Intermediate cardiac risk (generally <5%): carotid endarterectomy, intraperitoneal/intrathoracic surgery, orthopedic surgery, prostate surgery. Endoscopic/superficial/cataract are low; emergency major elderly is high.
hy: Intermediate: CEA, intraperitoneal/intrathoracic, orthopedic, prostate.

stem: Beyond chronic kidney disease, which combination is listed among risk factors for postoperative AKI?

options.A: Higher ASA grade, heart failure, nephrotoxic medications/contrast, cardiac surgery with cardiopulmonary bypass, and emergency surgery
options.B: Youth, low BMI, and elective minor surgery
options.C: Regional anesthesia and short operative time
options.D: Restrictive transfusion practice alone

exp: AKI risk factors include higher ASA grade, heart failure, nephrotoxic medications/contrast, cardiac surgery with cardiopulmonary bypass, and emergency surgery (plus age, obesity, diabetes, hypertension, hypotension, anemia, transfusion, CKD).
hy: AKI risks: high ASA, heart failure, nephrotoxins/contrast, CPB, emergency surgery.

stem: Which urinary-sodium and fractional-excretion pattern indicates intrinsic renal acute kidney injury?

options.A: Urinary sodium >50 mOsm/L and FeNa >3%, with urine osmolality near plasma
options.B: Urinary sodium <20 mOsm/L and FeNa <1%
options.D: BUN:creatinine ratio >20 with concentrated urine

exp: Intrinsic renal AKI shows urinary sodium >50 mOsm/L, FeNa >3%, and isosthenuric urine (≈ plasma), with BUN:creatinine <20. Prerenal is the opposite.
hy: Renal AKI: U-Na >50, FeNa >3%, isosthenuric urine.

stem: Which combination is listed as a risk factor for postoperative urinary retention?

options.A: Anticholinergic/opioid medications, benign prostatic hyperplasia, and colorectal or hernia surgery
options.B: Young age and laparoscopic appendectomy
options.C: Spinal anesthesia avoidance and early ambulation
options.D: Low fluid intake alone

exp: Urinary retention risk factors: anticholinergic/opioid medications, BPH, neurogenic bladder, diabetes, neurologic disorders, and colorectal/spine/hernia surgery.
hy: Retention risks: anticholinergics/opioids, BPH, colorectal/spine/hernia surgery.

stem: Besides tumors, which category and example is a recognized cause of SIADH?

options.A: CNS disorders (stroke, hemorrhage, infection, trauma) and drugs such as carbamazepine or SSRIs
options.B: Hyperthyroidism and Cushing disease
options.D: Hypercalcemia and hyperkalemia

exp: Beyond tumors (most often small cell lung cancer), SIADH is caused by CNS disorders (stroke, hemorrhage, infection, trauma), drugs (carbamazepine, SSRIs, cyclophosphamide), surgical stress, and endocrine disorders.
hy: SIADH causes: CNS disorders, drugs (carbamazepine/SSRIs), tumors, surgical stress.

stem: According to the table of frailty's pathophysiologic effects, which description is correct?

options.A: Frailty involves low testosterone/IGF-1/vitamin D, impaired immunity (low lymphocytes/CD8), a procoagulant state, and reduced GFR
options.B: Frailty raises testosterone and IGF-1 and improves immune function
options.C: Frailty produces an anticoagulant (bleeding) state with elevated plasminogen activator inhibitor-1
options.D: Frailty has no measurable effect on the cardiovascular response to anesthesia

exp: Frailty effects: low testosterone/IGF-1/vitamin D, impaired immunity (low lymphocytes/CD8), a procoagulant state (decreased PAI-1), reduced GFR/CYP450 activity, cognitive vulnerability, and cardiac autonomic dysfunction with labile BP under anesthesia.
hy: Frailty: low anabolic hormones, immune impairment, procoagulant state, reduced GFR, BP lability.

stem: Which of the following is a patient-related risk factor (not a protective factor) for surgical site infection?

options.A: High ASA score and diabetes mellitus
options.B: A laparoscopic operative approach
options.D: A short, elective, clean operation

exp: Patient-related SSI risk factors include high ASA score and diabetes (plus age, BMI, smoking, malnutrition, ascites, anemia, frailty). Laparoscopy and prophylaxis are protective; a short clean elective case is low-risk.
hy: High ASA and diabetes raise SSI risk.

stem: For surgical prophylaxis, what is the recommended adult cefazolin dose, including the adjustment for body weight?

options.A: 2 g, increased to 3 g for patients weighing >120 kg
options.B: 1 g for all adults regardless of weight
options.C: 500 mg, increased to 1 g for patients >100 kg
options.D: 4 g for every adult patient

exp: Prophylactic cefazolin is 2 g, increased to 3 g for patients >120 kg, to maintain adequate tissue concentration.
hy: Prophylactic cefazolin 2 g (3 g if >120 kg).

stem: Which prophylactic regimen is recommended for an elective colorectal operation?

options.A: Cefazolin plus metronidazole (or cefoxitin/cefotetan)
options.B: Cefazolin alone with no anaerobic coverage
options.D: Vancomycin monotherapy

exp: Colorectal prophylaxis requires anaerobic plus gram-negative coverage: cefazolin plus metronidazole, or cefoxitin/cefotetan (often with oral neomycin/erythromycin and a mechanical prep).
hy: Colorectal = cefazolin + metronidazole (or cefoxitin/cefotetan).

stem: For a clean head-and-neck operation that does not breach the mucosa, what does the prophylaxis table recommend?

options.A: No prophylaxis (none)
options.B: Carbapenem prophylaxis
options.D: Vancomycin plus an aminoglycoside

exp: A clean head-and-neck operation that does not enter the mucosa requires no prophylaxis; prophylaxis is reserved for prosthesis placement or clean-contaminated/cancer cases breaching mucosa.
hy: Clean head-and-neck without mucosal entry = no prophylaxis.

stem: During a long operation, intraoperative redosing of a prophylactic antibiotic is indicated under which circumstance?

options.A: When the duration of surgery exceeds two half-lives of the drug, or with massive blood loss
options.B: Every 30 minutes regardless of the agent used
options.C: Only after the operation has exceeded 8 hours
options.D: Redosing is never indicated once a preoperative dose is given

exp: Redose when the operative duration exceeds two half-lives of the drug, or with massive blood loss; shorten with rapid clearance and use caution in renal/hepatic impairment.
hy: Redose after ~2 half-lives or with major blood loss.

stem: Which of the following is classified as an infectious cause of postoperative fever?

options.A: Device-related (catheter) infection, pneumonia, and urinary tract infection
options.B: Atelectasis and dehydration
options.D: Pancreatitis and pulmonary embolus

exp: Infectious fever causes include device-related (catheter) infection, pneumonia, urinary tract infection, wound infection, abscess, and pseudomembranous colitis. Atelectasis, drug/transfusion reactions, pancreatitis, and PE are noninfectious.
hy: Infectious fever: device infection, pneumonia, UTI, wound, abscess.

stem: In treating myxedema coma, how are the associated hyponatremia and hypoventilation managed?

options.A: Hyponatremia with hypertonic (3%) saline and hypoventilation with mechanical ventilation
options.B: Hyponatremia with aggressive free-water boluses and hypoventilation with a respiratory stimulant
options.C: Hyponatremia with fluid restriction alone and hypoventilation with high-flow oxygen only
options.D: Hyponatremia with normal saline and hypoventilation with a beta agonist

exp: In myxedema coma, hyponatremia is treated with hypertonic (3%) saline and hypoventilation with mechanical ventilation; hypothermia with thyroid hormone and hypocortisolemia with IV hydrocortisone.
hy: Myxedema coma: 3% saline for Na; mechanical ventilation for hypoventilation.

stem: Which set are recognized late stomal complications?

options.A: Prolapse, stenosis, parastomal hernia, and parastomal varices
options.B: Ischemic necrosis, retraction, and detachment
options.C: Opening the wrong end and acute abscess formation
options.D: Immediate high output and acute peristomal dermatitis only

exp: Late stomal complications include prolapse, stenosis/stricture, parastomal hernia, fistula, and parastomal varices. Ischemic necrosis, retraction, detachment, and "opening the wrong end" are early.
hy: Late stoma: prolapse, stenosis, parastomal hernia, varices.

stem: Which is listed among the definitive (rather than merely implicated) factors in anastomotic leak?

options.A: Blood supply, tension on the suture line, and an airtight/watertight anastomosis
options.B: Mechanical bowel preparation
options.D: Patient gender

exp: Definitive anastomotic-leak factors are technical aspects, blood supply, tension on the suture line, an airtight/watertight anastomosis, and GI location; bowel prep, drains, gender, smoking, and steroids are merely "implicated."
hy: Definitive: technique, blood supply, tension, airtight anastomosis, location.

stem: Which set of features is unfavorable for spontaneous closure of an enterocutaneous fistula?

options.A: A short tract (<2 cm), an epithelialized tract, an end (vs lateral) fistula, and a large enteral defect (>1 cm)
options.B: A long tract (>2 cm) with a single, non-epithelialized channel
options.C: Low output with no adjacent abscess
options.D: Origin from the jejunum or colon with no distal obstruction

exp: Unfavorable ECF features: short tract (<2 cm), epithelialized tract, end (vs lateral) fistula, large enteral defect (>1 cm), adjacent abscess, distal obstruction, intrinsic bowel disease, malnutrition, and sepsis.
hy: Unfavorable ECF: short/epithelialized tract, end fistula, large defect, abscess, obstruction.

stem: According to the frailty-effects table, which cognitive/cardiovascular pairing is correct?

options.A: Cognition is impaired by anticholinergics and perioperative hypoxic injury amplified by surgery-induced cytokines (IL-1β, TNF-α); cardiac autonomic dysfunction causes blood-pressure lability under anesthesia
options.B: Cognition is unaffected, and the cardiovascular system shows enhanced autonomic stability
options.C: Frailty improves cytokine regulation and protects the brain during surgery
options.D: Frailty causes hypertensive surges (never hypotension) on induction of anesthesia

exp: Per the frailty-effects table, cognition is impaired by anticholinergics and perioperative hypoxic injury, amplified by surgery-induced cytokines (IL-1β, TNF-α); cardiac autonomic dysfunction causes blood-pressure lability under anesthesia.
hy: Frailty: cytokine/anticholinergic cognitive impairment + autonomic BP lability.

stem: What does the chapter identify as the most important predictor of traditional surgical outcomes in older adults?

options.A: Frailty (how successfully the patient has aged), rather than chronologic age alone
options.B: Chronologic age alone
options.D: The specific anesthetic agent used

exp: Although increasing age does worsen surgical outcomes, multiple studies confirm that age alone is not the major predictor of poor outcome — rather, it is how successfully the patient has aged. Frailty, not chronologic age, is now the most important predictor of traditional surgical outcomes. BMI (C) and anesthetic agent (D) are not the chapter's answer.
hy: Frailty, not chronologic age, best predicts surgical outcomes.
src: Sabiston Ch 13, "Outcomes of Surgery in Older Adults"

stem: Approximately what share of U.S. surgical procedures is accounted for by the ~15% of the population aged 65 and older?

options.A: About 40%
options.B: About 10%
options.D: About 65%

exp: The ~15% of the U.S. population aged 65+ accounts for about 40% of surgical procedures, and this share is rising as the Baby Boomers age. The other figures are incorrect. This demographic shift drives the need to standardize geriatric surgical care.
hy: 15% of the population (65+) → ~40% of surgical procedures.

stem: In studies of seriously ill older adults' treatment preferences (Fig. 13.2), what most strongly reduced willingness to undergo even a low-burden treatment?

options.A: The possibility of resulting functional or cognitive impairment
options.B: A small risk of death
options.C: The need for a brief hospital stay
options.D: The cost of the intervention

exp: In Fried's work on treatment preferences, even a small possibility of cognitive or functional decline sharply reduced patients' willingness to undergo treatment — far more than a small risk of death. Patients accepted high-risk-of-death treatments more readily than treatments risking functional/cognitive loss (B is the opposite). Stay/cost (C, D) were not the driver.
hy: Older patients fear functional/cognitive decline more than death.

stem: Per the life-expectancy table, roughly how many additional years can an average 65-year-old woman expect to live?

options.A: About 20.6 years
options.B: About 9.8 years
options.D: About 30 years

exp: Per Table 13.1, an average 65-year-old woman can expect ~20.6 more years (nearly double her 1900 counterpart). 9.8 years (B) is the 80-year-old figure. The increase in longevity brings more older adults living with surgically treatable chronic disease.
hy: 65-year-old woman ≈ 20.6 more years.

stem: Per the same table, roughly how many additional years can an average 80-year-old woman expect to live?

options.A: About 9.8 years
options.B: About 20.6 years
options.D: About 16 years

exp: Per Table 13.1, an average 80-year-old woman can expect ~9.8 more years; 20.6 years (B) is the 65-year-old figure. Life-expectancy estimates inform shared decision-making and screening decisions in older adults.
hy: 80-year-old woman ≈ 9.8 more years.

stem: The Fried frailty phenotype defines frailty by which five characteristics?

options.A: Weight loss, weak grip strength, self-reported exhaustion, slow walking speed, and low energy expenditure
options.B: Age, sex, ASA class, BMI, and serum albumin
options.C: Hypertension, diabetes, smoking, hyperlipidemia, and obesity
options.D: Cognition, mood, continence, vision, and hearing

exp: The Fried frailty phenotype defines frailty by weight loss, weak grip strength, self-reported exhaustion, slow walking speed, and low energy expenditure; ≥3 of these define the frail phenotype. The other lists are demographic/comorbidity or multidomain constructs, not the Fried phenotype.
hy: Fried phenotype: weight loss, weak grip, exhaustion, slow gait, low activity.

stem: Which set of cardiovascular changes accompanies normal aging?

options.A: Preserved systolic function but increased diastolic dysfunction, reduced β-adrenergic responsiveness, and greater dependence on preload (atrial kick)
options.B: Markedly reduced systolic function with preserved diastolic function
options.C: Enhanced β-adrenergic responsiveness with reduced reliance on preload
options.D: Decreased afterload from increased arterial compliance

exp: With aging, systolic function is generally preserved while diastolic dysfunction increases (responsible for up to 50% of heart failure in those >80); β-adrenergic responsiveness declines, and the heart depends more on preload (including atrial kick). Option B reverses systolic/diastolic, C reverses adrenergic/preload, and D is wrong because arterial compliance falls (afterload rises).
hy: Aging heart: preserved systole, more diastolic dysfunction, preload-dependent, hyposympathetic.

stem: Because the aging heart maintains cardiac output mainly by increasing ventricular filling (preload) rather than rate, what is a key clinical consequence?

options.A: Even minor hypovolemia can significantly compromise cardiac function
options.B: Tachycardia reliably compensates for blood loss
options.C: Atrial fibrillation has no hemodynamic effect
options.D: Cardiac output rises sharply with catecholamine infusion

exp: Because the aging heart maintains output by augmenting preload rather than rate, even minor hypovolemia (or loss of atrial kick) can significantly compromise cardiac function. Tachycardia does not reliably compensate (B), atrial fibrillation removes the atrial contribution to preload (C), and the heart is relatively unresponsive to catecholamines (D).
hy: Preload-dependent aging heart → minor hypovolemia is poorly tolerated.

stem: Which change in gas exchange and ventilatory control accompanies aging?

options.A: A decline in PaO₂ (~0.3–0.4 mm Hg/yr) with unchanged PaCO₂, and blunted ventilatory responses to hypoxia (~50%) and hypercapnia (~40%)
options.B: A decline in PaCO₂ with rising PaO₂
options.C: Enhanced ventilatory responses to hypoxia and hypercapnia
options.D: A large fall in total lung capacity

exp: Aging lowers PaO₂ by ~0.3–0.4 mm Hg/yr (ventilation-perfusion mismatch) while PaCO₂ stays unchanged, and ventilatory responses to hypoxia and hypercapnia fall by ~50% and ~40%, respectively. Options B and C reverse these, and total lung capacity actually stays roughly unchanged (D).
hy: Aging lung: ↓PaO₂, unchanged PaCO₂; blunted hypoxic (50%)/hypercapnic (40%) drive.

stem: How do lung volumes change with normal aging?

options.A: Total lung capacity is unchanged, but increased residual volume reduces vital capacity
options.B: Total lung capacity falls markedly while residual volume decreases
options.C: Vital capacity increases as residual volume falls
options.D: All lung volumes decrease proportionally

exp: Total lung capacity remains essentially unchanged with aging, but loss of elastic recoil increases residual volume, which reduces vital capacity. The other options misstate the volume changes; the loss of elastic inward recoil is partly offset by reduced chest-wall outward force.
hy: Aging: TLC unchanged, ↑residual volume → ↓vital capacity.

stem: Which statement about renal function in aging is correct?

options.A: GFR falls ~45% by age 80 and renal blood flow ~50%, yet serum creatinine may stay normal because lean body mass (and creatinine production) also falls
options.B: Serum creatinine reliably reflects GFR in the elderly
options.C: Renal blood flow increases with age
options.D: Tubular concentrating ability improves with age

exp: GFR falls ~45% by age 80 and renal blood flow ~50%, yet serum creatinine can remain normal because declining lean body mass reduces creatinine production — so creatinine is an unreliable marker of GFR in the elderly. Renal blood flow falls (C), and concentrating ability declines (D).
hy: Aging kidney: GFR ↓45%, RBF ↓50%; "normal" creatinine masks the decline.

stem: A serum creatinine of 1.0 mg/dL means very different things at different ages. Which statement and preferred estimating equation are correct?

options.A: It may represent CrCl >100 mL/min at age 30 but <60 mL/min at age 85; the Cockcroft-Gault equation correlates best with measured CrCl in older inpatients
options.B: It always represents the same CrCl regardless of age
options.C: The MDRD equation is preferred because 24-hour urine collection is easy in the elderly
options.D: Serum creatinine overestimates renal function only in young patients

exp: Because creatinine production falls with lean mass, a creatinine of 1.0 mg/dL can mean CrCl >100 mL/min at 30 but <60 mL/min at 85; the Cockcroft-Gault equation correlates best with measured CrCl in older inpatients (24-hour collection is unreliable due to incontinence/cognitive impairment). Options B–D misstate this.
hy: Same creatinine = very different CrCl by age; use Cockcroft-Gault.

stem: Which hepatobiliary change accompanies aging?

options.A: Decreased hepatocyte number and hepatic blood flow with preserved synthetic capacity, plus an increased incidence of gallstones
options.B: A marked fall in synthetic capacity (albumin, clotting factors)
options.D: A decreased incidence of gallstones

exp: Aging decreases hepatocyte number and hepatic blood flow and alters drug clearance, but synthetic capacity (albumin, clotting factors) remains essentially unchanged; gallstone incidence rises. Option B is wrong (synthesis is preserved), C reverses blood flow, and D reverses gallstone incidence.
hy: Aging liver: ↓hepatocytes/blood flow, preserved synthesis, ↑gallstones.

stem: Which set of immune changes (immunosenescence) accompanies aging?

options.A: Thymic involution with decreased naïve T-cell production, plus increased inflammatory cytokines and autoantibodies
options.B: Thymic enlargement with increased naïve T cells
options.D: Enhanced response to novel antigens

exp: Immunosenescence features thymic involution with decreased production of naïve T cells, decreased T-cell mitogenic activity, and increases in inflammatory cytokines and autoantibodies (autoantibody prevalence >10% by age 80). This leaves older hosts less able to respond to new antigens (D is wrong).
hy: Immunosenescence: thymic involution, fewer naïve T cells, more cytokines/autoantibodies.

stem: What perioperative blood-glucose target is now widely accepted, even in older patients?

options.A: Below 180 mg/dL
options.B: Below 110 mg/dL (tight control)
options.D: Below 80 mg/dL

exp: Maintaining blood glucose below 180 mg/dL in the perioperative period is now widely accepted, even in older patients; very tight control (<110) has not shown consistent benefit and risks hypoglycemia. The other targets are incorrect.
hy: Perioperative glucose goal <180 mg/dL (avoid overly tight control).

stem: In aging, "glucose intolerance" is characterized by a normal fasting glucose but a postchallenge glucose in which range?

options.A: >140 but <200 mg/dL
options.B: >200 mg/dL
options.D: >300 mg/dL

exp: Glucose intolerance in aging is defined by a normal fasting glucose with a postchallenge glucose >140 but <200 mg/dL (a value ≥200 would be diabetes). It reflects declining insulin secretion and/or increasing insulin resistance.
hy: Glucose intolerance: postchallenge 140–200 mg/dL with normal fasting glucose.

stem: In the study by Fried and colleagues, what proportion of older patients would NOT want even a low-burden treatment if severe functional or cognitive impairment was the expected outcome?

options.A: More than 70%
options.B: About 25%
options.D: Fewer than 5%

exp: More than 70% of older patients in Fried's study would not want even a low-burden treatment (e.g., IV antibiotics) if the expected outcome was severe functional or cognitive impairment — a concern that outweighed the concern for death. The smaller figures are incorrect.
hy: >70% refuse even low-burden treatment if functional/cognitive impairment is expected.

stem: The "Best Case/Worst Case" model and structured communication frameworks are designed primarily to do what?

options.A: Place the acute surgical condition in the context of the patient's overall health and goals to support goal-concordant shared decision-making
options.B: Estimate 30-day operative mortality with a validated calculator
options.C: Replace the need for an advance directive
options.D: Determine antibiotic prophylaxis

exp: The "Best Case/Worst Case" and structured communication models place the acute surgical problem in the context of the patient's overall health and goals, eliciting priorities and framing options (including palliative ones) to support goal-concordant shared decision-making. They are not mortality calculators (B), do not replace advance directives (C), and are unrelated to prophylaxis (D).
hy: Structured communication → goal-concordant shared decisions.

stem: Which statement about advance directives in older surgical patients is correct?

options.A: All patients should be encouraged to make a formal advance directive and name a surrogate; preferences should be discussed directly with the patient since surrogate–patient concordance is often poor
options.B: Surrogates reliably know the patient's wishes, so direct discussion is unnecessary
options.C: Advance directives preclude any disease-directed treatment
options.D: They are only relevant once the patient is already incapacitated

exp: All patients should be encouraged to complete a formal advance directive and name a surrogate, and preferences should be discussed directly with the patient because surrogate–patient (and physician–patient) concordance about wishes is often poor. Advance directives do not preclude disease treatment (C).
hy: Encourage advance directives; ask the patient directly — surrogates often misjudge wishes.

stem: The ACS/AGS best-practice preoperative assessment for geriatric patients is built around what?

options.A: A structured checklist covering cognition, depression, delirium risk, substance use, cardiac/pulmonary risk, functional status/falls, frailty, and nutrition
options.B: A single frailty questionnaire
options.C: Routine whole-body CT for all patients
options.D: Mandatory preoperative ICU admission

exp: The ACS/AGS best-practice guideline is a structured checklist covering cognition/capacity, depression, delirium risk, alcohol/substance use, cardiac and pulmonary risk, functional status and falls, frailty, nutrition, medication review/polypharmacy, and goals of care. It is not a single questionnaire (B) or routine imaging/ICU (C, D).
hy: ACS/AGS geriatric preop = multidomain checklist (cognition, falls, frailty, nutrition...).
src: Sabiston Ch 13, "Screening to Identify High-Risk Characteristics"

stem: In assessing older surgical patients, an inability to function above how many METs is associated with increased perioperative cardiac events and long-term risk?

options.A: 4 METs
options.B: 1 MET
options.D: 7 METs

exp: Exercise tolerance is the most sensitive predictor of perioperative cardiac/pulmonary complications, and an inability to exceed 4 METs is associated with increased perioperative cardiac events and long-term risk. One MET (3.5 mL/kg/min) is resting consumption; 10 METs is strenuous exertion.
hy: Inability to exceed 4 METs flags increased perioperative cardiac risk.

stem: On the Timed Up and Go test, which finding indicates a high fall risk?

options.A: Inability to rise from the chair without using the armrests, or a test time greater than 15 seconds
options.B: A test time under 5 seconds
options.C: Any need to turn around during the walk
options.D: Completing the test in under 30 seconds

exp: On the Timed Up and Go test, the inability to rise from the chair without using the armrests, or a test time greater than 15 seconds, indicates a high fall risk and prompts gait/balance training and physical therapy. The other findings are not the cutoff.
hy: TUG: can't rise without arms, or >15 s = high fall risk.

stem: Which combination is listed among factors associated with increased risk of malnutrition in older adults?

options.A: Recent weight loss, poor dentition, depression, polypharmacy, and swallowing disorders
options.B: High income and robust appetite
options.C: Young age and high physical activity
options.D: Recent weight gain and excellent oral intake

exp: Box 13.7 lists factors such as recent weight loss, limited ability to obtain food, immobility, poverty, depression, isolation, cognitive impairment, poor dentition, swallowing disorders, GERD, malabsorption, systemic disease, and appetite-suppressing or metabolism-blocking drugs. High income/good appetite, youth, and weight gain (B, C, D) are not risk factors.
hy: Malnutrition risks: weight loss, poor dentition, depression, polypharmacy, dysphagia.

stem: Which set of objective findings identifies an older patient at high risk for nutrition-related complications?

options.A: BMI <18.5 kg/m², serum albumin <3.0 g/dL, and unintentional weight loss >10%–15% within 6 months
options.B: BMI <25, albumin <4.5, and any weight loss
options.D: BMI <22, albumin <4.0, and 2% weight loss

exp: Severe nutritional deficits are flagged by BMI <18.5 kg/m², serum albumin <3.0 g/dL, and unintentional weight loss >10%–15% within 6 months — identifying high risk for nutrition-related complications and possibly warranting preoperative supplementation even if surgery is delayed. The other thresholds are incorrect.
hy: High nutritional risk: BMI <18.5, albumin <3.0, weight loss >10–15%/6 mo.

stem: According to the Beers list referenced in the chapter, which medications should generally be avoided in older adults?

options.A: Benzodiazepines, meperidine (Demerol), and diphenhydramine (Benadryl)
options.B: Acetaminophen, cefazolin, and lisinopril
options.D: Omeprazole, amlodipine, and levothyroxine

exp: The Beers list flags medications to avoid in older adults; the most commonly cited to avoid are all benzodiazepines, the analgesic meperidine (Demerol), and the antihistamine diphenhydramine (Benadryl). The other drug groups are not the Beers-list examples emphasized.
hy: Avoid in elderly (Beers): benzodiazepines, meperidine, diphenhydramine.

stem: The ACS Geriatric Surgery Verification standard for a multimodality pain plan emphasizes which approach?

options.A: Opioid-sparing techniques, "start low, go slow" titration, avoiding Beers-list drugs, and a prophylactic bowel regimen
options.B: High-dose scheduled opioids with benzodiazepine adjuncts
options.D: Withholding all analgesia to avoid delirium

exp: The ACS Geriatric Surgery Verification multimodality pain standard emphasizes opioid-sparing techniques (nonopioids, regional analgesia), "start low, go slow" titration for increased sensitivity, avoiding Beers-list drugs (benzodiazepines, meperidine, muscle relaxants), and a prophylactic bowel regimen. High-dose opioids/benzodiazepines (B), meperidine (C), and withholding analgesia (D) are wrong.
hy: Geriatric pain plan: opioid-sparing, start low/go slow, avoid Beers, bowel regimen.

stem: A positive Confusion Assessment Method (CAM) diagnosis of delirium requires which combination of features?

options.A: Acute onset with a fluctuating course AND inattention, plus either disorganized thinking or an altered level of consciousness
options.B: Chronic progressive memory loss over months
options.D: Hallucinations alone

exp: A positive CAM requires (1) acute onset with a fluctuating course AND (2) inattention, plus either (3) disorganized thinking or (4) an altered level of consciousness. Chronic progressive loss (B) describes dementia, a fixed deficit (C) is not delirium, and hallucinations alone (D) are insufficient.
hy: CAM: acute/fluctuating + inattention + (disorganized thinking OR altered consciousness).

stem: Which set of approximate postoperative delirium incidences by procedure is correct?

options.A: <5% after cataract surgery, ~35% after vascular surgery, 40%–60% after hip fracture repair, and >50% in ICU patients
options.B: ~50% after cataract surgery and <5% after hip fracture repair
options.D: Highest after cataract surgery and lowest after hip fracture repair

exp: Postoperative delirium incidence varies by procedure: <5% after cataract surgery, ~35% after vascular surgery, 40%–60% after hip fracture repair, and >50% in older ICU patients. The other options invert or flatten these rates.
hy: Delirium: cataract <5%, vascular 35%, hip fracture 40–60%, ICU >50%.

stem: Which is a predisposing (risk) factor — rather than a precipitating factor — for postoperative delirium?

options.A: Preexisting cognitive impairment
options.B: A new urinary tract infection
options.D: Acute electrolyte abnormality

exp: Predisposing (risk) factors are baseline patient characteristics — advanced age, cognitive/functional impairment, poor nutrition, comorbidity, sensory impairment — whereas precipitating factors are acute insults (infection, new medications, hypoxia, electrolyte disturbance, pain). Preexisting cognitive impairment is a predisposing factor; B, C, and D are precipitating factors.
hy: Predisposing = baseline (cognitive impairment); precipitating = acute insults (infection, drugs).

stem: Which is a precipitating factor for postoperative delirium, and which laboratory finding is associated with increased delirium risk?

options.A: Use of bladder catheters/restraints and sleep disruption are precipitating factors; a postoperative hematocrit <30% is associated with increased delirium risk
options.B: Advanced age is a precipitating factor; a hematocrit >45% increases risk
options.C: Baseline dementia is a precipitating factor; hypernatremia is protective
options.D: Epidural (vs general) anesthesia strongly precipitates delirium

exp: Precipitating factors include bladder catheters/restraints, sleep disruption, infection, medications, hypoxemia, and electrolyte abnormalities; a postoperative hematocrit <30% (along with intraoperative blood loss and transfusion) is associated with increased delirium risk. Notably, no association was found with anesthetic route (epidural vs general), so D is wrong.
hy: Precipitants: catheters/restraints, sleep disruption; hematocrit <30% raises delirium risk.

stem: Which statement about managing postoperative delirium is correct?

options.A: Prevention is best (orientation, mobility, sleep-wake cycle, avoiding deliriogenic drugs); low-dose antipsychotics (e.g., haloperidol) are reserved for patients who are a danger to themselves or others
options.B: Scheduled benzodiazepines are first-line therapy
options.C: General anesthesia clearly causes more delirium than epidural anesthesia
options.D: Physical restraints are the preferred first measure

exp: The best treatment for delirium is prevention (orientation, family at bedside, sensory aids, mobility, normal sleep-wake cycle, avoiding dehydration and deliriogenic drugs); low-dose antipsychotics such as haloperidol are reserved for patients who pose a danger to themselves or others. Benzodiazepines are not first-line (B), no association exists with anesthetic route (C), and restraints should be avoided (D).
hy: Delirium: prevent first; low-dose antipsychotic only for dangerous agitation.

stem: Which statement about postoperative aspiration in older adults is correct?

options.A: Incidence rises almost exponentially with age (patients >80 have a 9–10× higher risk than those 18–29); a 3-ounce bedside swallow test and 30–45° upright positioning help reduce risk
options.B: Aspiration risk is independent of age
options.D: A 3-ounce swallow test is used to diagnose pneumonia

exp: Postoperative aspiration-pneumonia incidence rises almost exponentially with age — patients >80 have a 9- to 10-fold higher risk than those 18–29 — and a 3-ounce bedside swallow test plus 30–45° upright positioning (during and after meals) helps reduce risk. Aspiration risk is age-dependent (B), supine positioning worsens risk (C), and the swallow test assesses swallowing, not pneumonia (D).
hy: >80 yr → 9–10× aspiration risk; 3-oz swallow test + 30–45° upright.

stem: Which statement about deconditioning from immobility is correct?

options.A: Functional decline can occur after as little as 2 days of immobility, and the period for functional recovery may be up to 3 times longer than the period of immobility
options.B: Functional decline requires at least 2 weeks of bed rest
options.C: Recovery is always faster than the immobility period
options.D: Bed rest has no effect on bone or muscle

exp: Functional decline can occur after as little as 2 days of immobility, and the period needed for functional recovery may be up to 3 times longer than the period of immobility — underscoring the value of early mobilization. The other options understate or deny these effects.
hy: Decline after 2 days' immobility; recovery up to 3× the immobile period.

stem: Which cardiovascular/respiratory effects of bed rest are listed in the chapter?

options.A: Decreased stroke volume and cardiac output with orthostatic hypotension, and decreased respiratory excursion with increased atelectasis potential
options.B: Increased stroke volume and cardiac output
options.C: Improved oxygen uptake and reduced atelectasis
options.D: No measurable cardiovascular effect

exp: Table 13.3 lists decreased stroke volume and cardiac output with orthostatic hypotension (cardiovascular) and decreased respiratory excursion/oxygen uptake with increased atelectasis potential (respiratory). Options B–D reverse or deny these effects.
hy: Bed rest: ↓stroke volume/CO + orthostasis; ↓respiratory excursion + atelectasis.

stem: Beyond the heart and lungs, which musculoskeletal and other effects of bed rest are listed?

options.A: Decreased muscle strength/blood flow, increased bone loss/decreased bone density, constipation, incontinence, and skin breakdown
options.B: Increased muscle strength and bone density
options.D: Enhanced skin integrity

exp: Table 13.3 also lists decreased muscle strength and blood flow, increased bone loss with decreased bone density, gastrointestinal effects (malnutrition, anorexia, constipation), genitourinary incontinence, skin breakdown, and psychological effects (isolation, depression, disorientation). Options B–D contradict these.
hy: Bed rest: ↓muscle/bone, constipation, incontinence, skin breakdown.

stem: Which statement about thyroid nodules/cancer in older adults is correct?

options.A: Malignant nodules tend to have more aggressive phenotypes in the elderly (thyroid-cancer–specific mortality ~8%), and outcomes are better when the surgeon's volume exceeds ~30 thyroidectomies/year
options.B: Thyroid cancers are uniformly indolent in the elderly
options.C: Surgeon volume has no effect on outcomes
options.D: Age is a favorable prognostic factor for thyroid cancer

exp: In older adults, thyroid nodules are common but malignant nodules tend to be more aggressive, with thyroid-cancer–specific mortality ~8%; outcomes in complex older patients are better when the surgeon performs >30 thyroidectomies/year. Thyroid cancers are not uniformly indolent (B), surgeon volume matters (C), and age is a negative prognostic factor (D).
hy: Elderly thyroid cancer: more aggressive (~8% mortality); high-volume surgeon better.

stem: In an older outpatient with hypercalcemia, which statement is correct?

options.A: Primary hyperparathyroidism is the most common cause; surgery is indicated for serum calcium >1 mg/dL above normal, 24-h urine calcium >400 mg, CrCl <60 mL/min, or a T-score below −2.5
options.B: Malignancy is the most common cause of outpatient hypercalcemia
options.C: Surgery is never indicated for asymptomatic disease
